title: Quantum oscillations and nonsaturating magnetoresistivity in nodal-line semimetals
abstract: Understanding the magnetotransport behaviors in topological systems remains alluring, as a lot of intrinsic information could be extracted, e.g., the band structures, Berry phase, Fermi surface, carrier density, and so on. Motivated by the recent magnetotransport developments in nodal-line semimetal, EuGa4, in this paper, we will study the magnetotransport properties of the system, focusing on the quantum oscillations and nonsaturating magnetoresistivity (MR). Firstly, we analyze the chemical potential and magnetoconductivity oscillations with the magnetic field and reveal that there exist two distinct oscillation frequencies, which are caused by the characteristic torus Fermi surface and can be regarded as an important experimental signature of nodal-line semimetals. Then we calculate the MR and find that although the MR is nonsaturating with the magnetic field in the low-energy region, the MR ratio is much smaller than that reported in the experiment.
summary: This paper provides a theoretical framework to identify nodal-line semimetals by predicting two distinct quantum oscillation frequencies arising from a torus Fermi surface. While it successfully identifies a topological signature, it highlights a gap between current theory and experiment regarding the magnitude of magnetoresistivity.
main_problem: The study aims to identify unambiguous experimental signatures of nodal-line semimetals through magnetotransport properties, specifically focusing on quantum oscillations and nonsaturating magnetoresistivity.
main_result: The authors identify two distinct oscillation frequencies in the low-energy regime as a signature of the torus Fermi surface, though they find a significant discrepancy in the magnetoresistivity ratio compared to experimental values.
method: A full quantum mechanical study using Landau quantization and the Kubo-Bastin formula to calculate conductivity tensors and chemical potential evolution.
model_or_system: A 3D Hamiltonian model for nodal-line semimetals (motivated by EuGa4) featuring a torus-like Fermi surface and a Dirac mass term that breaks PT symmetry.
key_observables: Magnetoconductivity oscillations, magnetoresistivity (MR) ratio, Hall conductivity, and chemical potential oscillations.
important_parameters: Low-energy vs. high-energy regimes, Dirac mass (M), carrier density (n0), magnetic field (B), and impurity scattering rate (eta).
main_assumptions: The model uses a simplified 1D band/torus model, assumes the zero-temperature limit, and neglects spin Zeeman splitting due to its small magnitude.
figures_summary: Fig 1(a) shows 2D bands in k-space; Fig 2(a) displays Landau level spectra showing nonmonotonic energy shifts; Fig 3(a) shows the evolution of chemical potential.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the motivation from EuGa4, defines the Hamiltonian and torus model, presents the quantum mechanical derivation of Landau levels, analyzes conductivity and oscillations using the Kubo-Bastin formula, and concludes by discussing the discrepancy in MR ratios and potential future improvements.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (Color online) (a) The 2D bands of nodal-line semimetals in the (¯kx, ¯ky) space with ¯kz = 0. (b) The DOS ¯D(¯ε) and carrier density ¯n0(¯µ). Note the double y-axis in the figure. The DOS shows a singularity at the transition point ¯ε = ¯εt, as indicated by the arrow. (c) The simplified 1D bands vs ¯k∥, with ¯kz = 0. The left and right insets show the 3D Fermi surface in the momentum space, in which the Fermi energy ¯ε = 0.8 and ¯ε = 1.4, respectively. Note that the extremal circular orbits, α and β, are denoted by the black lines.

FIG. 2. (Color online) (a) The LL spectra vs the magnetic field ¯B. The arrow indicates the LL index growing from n = 0 up to n = 9. The dotted lines label the critical fields ¯B = ¯Bc1 and ¯B = ¯Bc2. (b) The LL dispersion vs ¯kz when ¯B = 0.106, with the LL index n being labeled. In (a) and (b), each LL owns a definite even or odd parity that are represented by the solid or dashed lines, respectively.

FIG. 3. (Color online) (a) The evolution of the chemical po- tential ¯µ with the inverse magnetic field ¯B−1. The red and green lines are under fixed carrier densities ¯n0 = 0.02042 and 0.08946, respectively. The LL spectra are also plotted, with the index from n = 0 up to n = 18, in which the even and odd parities are represented by the solid and dashed lines, respec- tively. (b)-(c) The FFT analysis of the chemical potential.

FIG. 4. (Color online) The longitudinal conductivity ¯σxx in (a) and (c), and the Hall conductivity ¯σxy in (b) and (d) vs the inverse magnetic field ¯B−1. The different lines represent the different linewidths ¯η. Note that ¯η can take zero in (b) and (d), but not in (a) and (c). The carrier density is fixed as ¯n0 = 0.08946 in (a)-(b), and ¯n0 = 0.02042 in (c)-(d). In (a) and (c) insets, we show the FFT analysis of ¯σxx at ¯η = 0.01 and ¯η = 0.02. The legends are the same in all figures.

FIG. 5. (Color online) The longitudinal MR ¯ρxx vs the mag- netic field ¯B. Note that the double log-axis is used. The different lines represent the different linewidths ¯η. In (a) and (b), ¯ρxx is calculated as ¯ρxx = ¯σ−1 xx , while in (c) and (d), ¯ρxx is calculated by using Eq. (21). The carrier density is fixed as ¯n0 = 0.02042 in (a) and (c), and ¯n0 = 0.08946 in (b) and (d). The legends are the same in all figures.
